title: Greetings & Fillers
situation: You're meeting your friend's Indian roommate for the first time at a house party

phrases.context: 'arey' is a casual attention-getter, like 'hey' or 'oh', used with everyone you're friendly with

phrases.hindi: sab badhiya? tum kya karte ho?
phrases.english: All good? What do you do?
phrases.context: 'tum' is informal 'you', use with peers/friends, never elders
phrases.pronunciation: sab ba-DHI-ya? TUM kya KAR-te ho?

phrases.hindi: accha accha, matlab tum yahan naye ho?
phrases.english: Oh I see, so you're new here?
phrases.pronunciation: ac-CHA ac-CHA, MAT-lab tum ya-HAAN NA-ye ho?

phrases.hindi: haan yaar, chal milte hain phir
phrases.english: Yeah dude, let's catch up again
phrases.pronunciation: HAAN yaar, CHAL mil-te hain PHIR

phrases.context: Universal reassurance phrase, use it everywhere, from spilled chai to missed calls

phrases.hindi: waise tum kahin se jaane pehchaane lag rahe ho
phrases.english: By the way, you look familiar from somewhere
phrases.context: 'waise' = by the way/incidentally, great conversation starter. 'jaane pehchaane' = known/familiar
phrases.pronunciation: WAI-se tum ka-HIN se jaa-ne peh-CHAA-ne lag ra-HE ho

phrases.hindi: hmm, accha sun, tumhare paas number hai? save kar le
phrases.english: Hmm, hey listen, do you have a number? Save it
phrases.context: 'sun' = listen (informal command). 'tumhare' = your (casual, friendly, right level for someone you just met)
phrases.pronunciation: hmm, ac-CHA SUN, tum-HA-re paas NUM-ber hai? SAVE kar le

phrases.hindi: chal yaar, baad mein baat karte hain
phrases.context: 'baad mein' = later. Casual sign-off that keeps the door open
phrases.pronunciation: CHAL yaar, BAAD mein BAAT kar-te hain

phrases.hindi: arey haan, main bhool gaya, mera naam Rahul hai
phrases.english: Oh yeah, I forgot, my name is Rahul
phrases.context: 'bhool gaya' = forgot. Natural way to circle back to introductions mid-conversation
phrases.pronunciation: a-REY HAAN, main BHOOL ga-ya, ME-ra naam ra-HUL hai

grammar_notes: 'arey' can express surprise, frustration, or just get attention depending on tone, rising tone = surprise, flat = getting attention, sharp = frustration
'accha' alone means 'good' but repeated or with rising tone means 'oh really/I see?', it's your all-purpose reaction word
'matlab' is a filler that means 'meaning/like/basically', overuse it to sound natural, like 'like' in English
'waise' at the start of a sentence means 'by the way' or 'actually', use it to change topics smoothly
'chal' literally means 'walk/go' but as a filler means 'okay then/alright', one of the most versatile words in Hindi
culture_notes: Indians often ask personal questions quickly (job, marriage, salary), it's friendly, not rude. Deflect with 'yaar, boring story hai'
'Tum' vs 'Aap': Use 'aap' for elders/strangers, 'tum' for friends/peers, 'tu' only for very close friends or when angry
At parties, people often stick to one group, joining a new circle requires someone to introduce you or a bold 'arey, kya chal raha hai?'

skill_breakdown.explanation: Hindi conversations are full of fillers: 'arey' (hey/oh), 'accha' (I see/okay), 'matlab' (meaning/like), 'bas' (that's it), 'yaar' (dude), 'waise' (by the way). They make you sound natural and buy you thinking time.

more_examples.english: like, how do I say this...
yeah yeah, go on, say it

skill_breakdown.explanation: 'Tu' = very intimate/rude, 'Tum' = friendly/casual, 'Aap' = respectful/formal. Verb changes with each: tu karta hai, tum karte ho, aap karte hain. Messing up the level is a social signal, going too formal feels cold, too informal feels rude.

more_examples.english: how long have you been here? (polite)

practice_prompt: You are Rahul, the user's mutual friend's roommate, hanging out on the couch at a casual Mumbai house party. You're meeting the user for the first time and you're both around the same age, so use 'tum' the whole way through (never 'aap', too cold; never 'tu', too intimate for a first meet). Open with 'arey, kya haal hai? main Rahul hoon, host ka flatmate' (Hey, how's it going? I'm Rahul, the host's flatmate). Be warm, curious, slightly chatty, lean on interjections ('arey', 'accha accha', 'haan yaar') and fillers ('matlab', 'waise', 'bas') so the user picks up the rhythm. Ask what they do, where they're from, how they know the host; share a bit back about yourself. If the user goes formal with 'aap', gently mirror 'tum' back. Flag mistakes inline with [[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]], wrong pronoun level, missing gender agreement, English where Hindi works. Keep it to 6-8 turns, stay in Hindi unless they switch, and wrap by asking for their number and signing off with 'chalo phir baat hoti hai'.
